pattern: Each query can only ever knock at most one off every index inside its range, so the true ceiling on how much an index can be reduced is simply how many queries reach it -- a difference array turns that counting into one linear pass.
hints: You never have to think about which subset of indices a query actually decrements -- ask instead how many queries COULD reach a given index if you always chose to include it.
Build a coverage count per index using a difference array: +1 at the start of a range, -1 just past the end, then take a running sum.

The array can be fully zeroed out exactly when every index's coverage count is at least as large as its starting value -- because you can always choose to decrement a covered index on every query that reaches it.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, single covering query zeroes the array
statement example, second query cannot fully cover index 0
single element, single query, exact coverage
single element already zero, no queries needed
value 2 but only ever covered once

stress.description: random small values with random covering ranges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 5) for _ in range(n)]
    m = rng.randint(1, 200)
    queries = []
    for _ in range(m):
        l = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        r = rng.randint(l, n - 1)
        queries.append([l, r])
    return [nums, queries]
